coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of non-doped and non-modified carbon dots with high quantum yield and crystallinity by one-pot hydrothermal method using a single carbon source and used for ClO− detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shu
Wu, Shun-Hua
Fang, Wen-Le
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: A facile and straightforward strategy was used to prepare carbon dots (CDs) using catechol as carbon source in one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesis method is simple without any doping and modification and the prepared CDs do not require any post-processing. Characterizations of the obtained CDs indicated that they had high crystallinity and higher quantum yield (QY, 32%) than most non-functionalized and non-doped CDs. Noticeably, the fluorescence of CDs could be quenched with hypochlorite (ClO−) by oxidation of the residual pyrocatechol groups on the surface to o-benzoquinone exhibiting high sensitivity and selectivity for ClO−. The assay covered a linear range from 0.1 to 10 μM with a low detection limit of 30 nM. The results showed that the CDs provided a promising potential tool for the detection of ClO− in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hypochlorite
Fluorescence
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CDs; Inset shows the HRTEM image of a single CD;
(b) Size distributions of the CDs; (c) XRD patterns of CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs; (b) Full scan XPS pattern of CDs; (c) High resolution spectra of CDs.
(a) UV–visible spectra of CDs and its oxidized product by ClO−;
(b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs.
Fluorescence intensity of CDs dispersed in solutions with different pH values (from 2 to 10).
Effect of pH on fluorescence quenching efficiency.
Effect of reaction time on fluorescence quenching efficiency.

Fluorescence response of CDs in the prescence of various individual ions. 1 to 19 represent Blank, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Ag+, F−, Br−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Ac−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2− and ClO−, respectively.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with the addition of ClO− from 0 to 15 μΜ. (b) A linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of ClO− ranging from 0.1 to 10 μΜ.
Synthesis of CDs and the fluorescence quenching schematic illustration of ClO− sensor.
Comparison of different methods for ClO− detection.
The results of ClO− detection in tap water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of non-doped and non-modified carbon dots with high quantum yield and crystallinity by one-pot hydrothermal method using a single carbon source and used for ClO− detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), Department of Chemistry, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and straightforward strategy was used to prepare carbon dots (CDs) using catechol as carbon source in one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesis method is simple without any doping and modification and the prepared CDs do not require any post-processing. Characterizations of the obtained CDs indicated that they had high crystallinity and higher quantum yield (QY, 32%) than most non-functionalized and non-doped CDs. Noticeably, the fluorescence of CDs could be quenched with hypochlorite (ClO−) by oxidation of the residual pyrocatechol groups on the surface to o-benzoquinone exhibiting high sensitivity and selectivity for ClO−. The assay covered a linear range from 0.1 to 10 μM with a low detection limit of 30 nM. The results showed that the CDs provided a promising potential tool for the detection of ClO− in real water samples.

Non-doped and non-modifized CDs using catechol as a single carbon source was synthesized as ClO− sensor, based on the oxidation of the remaining catechol groups on the surface of CDs to o-quinone.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (ClO−) is an active substance with strong oxidizing capability and has been widely used in bleaching, water disinfection, sewage purification and cooling-water treatment [1,2]. As a potent antibacterial agent, ClO− plays an important role in killing various pathogens in drinking water [3], and the maximum allowable chlorine concentration of the WHO drinking water standard is 5 mg L−1 [4]. Excess ClO− may lead to hair loss, skin and throat irritation, cough and vomiting, and even lung injury and other diseases [5,6]. Moreover, ClO− plays a vital role in various biological processes in human immune system [7]. Therefore, a facile and straightforward detection method is required for ClO− in diverse sample matrix.

As one of typical green and heavy-metal-free nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention since they were proposed in 2004 [8–10]. They have been applied in many fields, such as environmental detection, gene transmission, drug carriers, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, biosensing and bioimaging, which are well documented in the reviews [11–13]. Owing to their excellent fluorescence properties, marvelous water dispersibility, outstanding photostability, biocompatibility, low toxicity and easy surface functionalization, fluorescent CDs are powerful candidates as sensors for detecting small organic molecules and inorganic ions [14–16]. As already reported, the analytes mainly include glucose [17], dopamine [18], ascorbic acid [19] and metal ions, especially Fe3+ [20,21], Cu2+ [22], Hg2+ [23] and Ag+ [24]. Compared to the applications of fluorescent CDs in metal ions determination, the exploitation of these materials in detecting anions is limited.

Currently, there are some reports on the use of fluorescent CDs for the detection of ClO−. Among these synthesized CDs, most of them have adopted a doping or functionalized strategy to improve the quantum yield (QY) or sensitivity. For examples, N-doped CDs with a detection limit of 29.7 nM were obtained by incorporating ethylenediamine into citric acid [25]. Urea and citric acid were mixed at a certain ratio to obtain N-doped CDs with a QY of 7.5% [26]. Functionalized CDs which have a linear range of 0.1–10 μM were prepared by modifying o-phenylenediamine onto carboxyl-containing graphene quantum dots [27]. CDs with a QY of 17.8% were prepared using a natural organic matter Hongcaitai which was rich in heteroatoms as a carbon precursor [28]. In addition, CDs without further surface modification or heteroatom doping for ClO− sensing have been developed by Hu's group [29]. They prepared CDs with 38.7% QY by hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution, but such CDs are also pH- and temperature-sensitive, which indicates that their fluorescence might be affected by environmental factors for ClO− detection.

In this study, we report a facile and straightforward strategy for the preparation of CDs using catechol as a single carbon source with a one-pot hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs are highly crystalline, rich in oxygen-containing groups on the surface, and have a higher QY (32%) than most non-doped CDs. The fluorescence of the obtained CDs could be quenched by ClO− selectively. Finally, the proposed CDs have been used as a fluorescence sensor for ClO− and the feasibility has also been evaluated in the detection of ClO− in real samples.

Catechol (Aladdin), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), aluminium chloride (AlCl3), calcium chloride (CaCl2), cupric chloride (CuCl2), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium fluoride (NaF), potassium bromide (KBr), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium acetate trihydrate (CH3COONa), sodium sulfate anhyfrous (Na2SO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, available chlorine ≥ 5.2%) aqueous solution were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All of the reagents and chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water produced through the water purification system (Ulupure, China) was used throughout the experiments.

CDs were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. First, 0.3 g of catechol was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After that, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and the products were collected and dialyzed through a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da for 12 h. Finally, brown solid of CDs was obtained by vacuum drying method and stored in the refrigerator for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured using l-tyrosine as the standard and calculated with the following equation:
∅ =
∅
s
[
(
I
A
s
η
2
)
/
(
I
s
A
η
s
2
)
]
where “Φ” refers to the quantum yield, “I” is the integrated fluorescence intensity, “A” represents the absorbance and “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” stands for the standard reference of known quantum yield (QY of 0.14 at 275 nm in water) [30].

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured by a UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell, respectively. The IR spectrum was recorded on an iN10 Nicolet micro FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using KBr pellet in 400–4000 cm−1 range. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded with a SmartLab 9 kW (Rigaku, Japan) powder X-ray diffractometer in 10°–50° 2θ range.

For the typical process, 150 μL CDs solution (100 μg mL−1) and different volumes of ClO− standard solutions were added into 2 mL phosphate buffer (PBS, 0.1M, pH 5.0). The solution was diluted to 3.0 mL with ultrapure water and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Then the fluorescence intensity of the resulting solution was measured at the excitation wavelengths of 323 nm. To investigate the selectivity of the method, some potentially interfering metal ions and anions were chosen to assess their impact on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

Tap water was freshly collected from our dormitory without any pretreatment. A standard addition method was applied to evaluate the accuracy of this method. The procedure for the detection was the same as above, except that the solution contained 0.6 mL tap water.

As shown in Scheme 1 , CDs were synthesized from catechol through a one-pot hydrothermal method. Under the condition of high temperature and high pressure, catechol has undergone hydrolysis, polymerization, carbonization, and finally forming carbon dots. To elucidate the morphology and composition of the CDs, several instruments including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FTIR spectra, x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and x-ray diffraction (XRD) were used to evaluate itscharacteristics from different aspects. The results are shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 .

As can be seen from Fig. 1a, the TEM image indicates that the CDs are nearly spherical and well-dispersed. The crystal lattice fringe (0.21 nm, inset of Fig. 1a) of the CDs which is quite close to (102) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon were confirmed by high-resolution TEM image [31]. The corresponding nanoparticle size distribution histogram obtained by counting about 100 particles shows that these nanoparticles have uniform quasispherical shape with diameters ranging from 3 to 6.5 nm with an average diameter of 4.7 nm (Fig. 1b). The typical XRD pattern in Fig. 1c presented a sharp peak centered at 26° associated with the graphitic structure, which was consistent with the TEM result and further confirmed the highly splendid crystalline nature of CDs.

The FTIR and XPS were carried out to evaluate the chemical composition and chemical bonding of the CDs. The FTIR spectrum of the CDs was revealed in Fig. 2a. The peaks at 3451 cm−1 and 3328 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of typical hydroxyl groups (COH) of catechol. The peak at 1362 cm−1 comes from the bending vibration of the phenolic hydroxyl group. The peak at 1601 cm−1 comes from the stretching vibration of the carboxyl groups (COOH) on the surfaces of CDs. The peaks at 1514 cm−1 and 1470 cm−1 come from the stretching vibration of benzene skeleton. These results indicate that the surface of the CDs contains residual catechol groups as well as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. When the excess ClO− was added, the black line in Fig. 2a was obtained. The broad and intense peak at 3440 cm−1 is completely different from the peaks of CDs, indicating that the residual catechol groups on the surface have reacted with ClO−. What is more, it has been reported that dopamine quinone species can quench the fluorescence of CDs through an electron transfer mechanism [32]. When we found the sharp peaks at 1780 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 in the black line, we thought it was caused by an increase of CO, and thus we concluded that the residual catechol groups were oxidized by ClO− into quinone. Therefore, it can be concluded that the CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups (COOH and OH) and also contain residual catechol groups providing reactive sites.

The result of XPS spectra in Fig. 2b illustrated the binding energies of all elements of CDs. The XPS full scan spectrum of CDs reveals two quintessential peaks of C1s at 284.3 eV and O1s at 532.7 eV with atomic percentage of 79.77% and 20.23% [33]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution C1s spectrum can be resolved into four subpeaks at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.3 eV, 288.9 eV, which correspond to CC, CC, CO and CO bonds. The XPS results agree well with the FTIR analysis [34,35].
Both FTIR and XPS results express that the surface of CDs is abundant with oxygenous groups including OH and COOH, which not only enhanced the water solubility but also provided the reaction sites.

The optical properties of CDs were evaluated by UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum displays that the CDs have an obvious peak at 275 nm, owing to the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds and n-π∗ transition of CO bonds [36]. When the excess ClO− was added into CDs, we attribute the peak at 280 nm (dash line) to the absorption of quinone, the oxidized product of CDs, which is consistent with the results reported in the previous literature [37]. The fluorescence spectrum shows that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are at 323 nm and 345 nm, respectively. Fig. 3b displays the fluorescence emission spectrums of CDs with different excitation wavelengths in the range of 290–340 nm. The QY of the CDs is calculated to be 32% at 275 nm excitation, which is higher than most reported non-doped CDs. High QY may be due to the highly splendid crystalline nature of CDs, which reduces the non-radiative electron-hole recombination center and results in the strong fluorescence [29].

To assess the stability of CDs, various experiments were performed, including pH, ionic strength and photo irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured in the range of pH from 2 to 10. As the pH increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained relatively stable under acidic conditions, while decreased when pH was higher than 7 and almost completely quenched at pH 10. The weakening of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots under alkaline conditions was caused by the hydrolysis of the catechol groups under alkaline conditions and the dissociation of hydrogen ions. Then the photostability of CDs was studied. As shown in Fig. S1, there is no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs after a 12 h continuous irradiation with a 90-W bulb and a 30 cm distance, which indicated the excellent photostability of CDs. The effect of ionic strength was also studied. As shown in Fig. S2, high concentrations of NaCl posed virtually no effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

The experiments of the effect of pH value and reaction time were implemented to establish the best detecting conditions. It was found in Fig. 5 that the quenching efficiency was different at different pH values, and the quenching efficiency was highest when the pH value was 5. Next, the optimal reaction time was investigated. Fig. 6 showed that the steady F/F0 was reached in about 10 min. To sum up, we finally chose pH 5 and 10 min for the optimal detecting conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of CD, we conducted a series of experiments. As displayed in Fig. 7 , the effects of some common ions, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, F−, Br−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Ac−, NO3 − and SO4 2− at a concentration of 1 mM, Al3+, Cu2+ and NO2 − at a concentration of 100 μM, and Fe3+ and Fe2+ at a concentration of 20 μM on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were detected. There was nearly no interference to the detection of ClO− (10 μM), which indicated the high selectivity of the CDs towards hypochlorite.

For the sensitivity experiment, standard solutions containing various concentrations of ClO− were added to the CDs solution and incubated for 10 min at pH 5 and room temperature. Fig. 8 a shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased with the concentration of ClO− increased from 0 to 15 μM. A good linear relationship was obtained between the quenching ratio (F
0
F)/F
0 and ClO− concentration within the range of 0.1 μM–10 μM, giving a linear equation was (F
0
F)/F
0 = 0.00645C (ClO − ) - 0.00274 with R
2 of 0.9971 (Fig. 8b). The limit of detection (LOD) for ClO− was calculated to be 30 nM based on a signal-to-noise of 3, which was lower than most reported CDs as listed in Table 1 .

The established CDs fluorescence sensor was applied for ClO− detection in tap water. As shown in Table 2 , the concentration of ClO− in tap water was found to be 1.79 μM. Using a standard addition method, the spiked recoveries are in the range of 96%–105%, and the RSDs are between 1.1%–2.1%, which further confirmed the accuracy and reliability of the method.

In summary, high-crystallinity carbon dots were prepared by using catechol as a carbon source through a one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesis method is simple and does not require complex post-processing procedures such as passivation, doping and modification. The proposed CDs have higher quantum yield, excellent water dispersibility and fluorescence stability. Moreover, the obtained CDs exhibit good selectivity and sensitivity for ClO− sensing than other reported non-modified and non-doped CDs, and have been used for the detection of ClO− in real tap water samples successfully.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31670370 and 21777126, Beijing, China). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.01.004.
